title: SORT
summary: SORT returns a range sorted by one or more columns, spilling a live ordered copy of your data without touching or rearranging the original.
jobUseCases: Show a top-to-bottom ranking of salespeople by revenue on a dashboard
Order a customer list alphabetically for a clean printed report
Sort tasks by due date so the most urgent appear first
Build a leaderboard that re-orders automatically as scores update
Present products from highest to lowest margin without altering the source table

examples.title: Sort ascending (default)
examples.explanation: By default SORT orders by the first column in ascending order. Given names and sales in A2:B50, it spills an alphabetically sorted copy, leaving your original list untouched.

examples.title: Sort by a specific column, descending
examples.explanation: The sort_index 2 sorts by the second column (Sales) and sort_order -1 makes it descending, so the highest sales appear first. Use 1 for ascending and -1 for descending.

examples.title: Sort by multiple columns
examples.explanation: Pass arrays to sort on more than one key. Here it sorts by column 3 ascending first, then by column 2 descending as a tie-breaker, useful for grouping by region then ranking sales within each.

examples.title: Sort the result of a FILTER
examples.explanation: Nest FILTER inside SORT to first keep only North-region rows, then order them by Sales descending. This single formula produces a clean, ranked, filtered table that updates live.

commonMistakes: Giving a sort_index larger than the number of columns in the array, which causes a #VALUE! error
Using a sort_order other than 1 or -1, which is invalid
Letting the spill area collide with existing content, producing a #SPILL! error
Expecting SORT to change the original data; it returns a separate sorted copy and leaves the source as-is
Mismatching the lengths of the sort_index and sort_order arrays when sorting by multiple columns
practiceTips: Remember sort_order is 1 (ascending) or -1 (descending), not TRUE/FALSE.
Use SORTBY when you want to sort one range by the values in a different range.
Reference an Excel Table so the sorted output grows automatically with new rows.
Combine with UNIQUE and FILTER to build clean, ordered, deduplicated lists in one formula.
